**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

**[Date: April 15, 1894]**  
**[Location: The Lighthouse at the End of the World, Isle of Dunmore]**

---

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with a hand that trembles—not from fear, but from the weight of what I must say. The wind howls like a banshee outside these walls, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the world whole. I do not know if I will live to see the dawn. But if I do, I must tell you this: I am sorry.

Five years have passed since the last time we spoke, and five years is a long time for a man to carry a silence heavier than stone. I have not spoken to you in five years, Clara. Not a word. Not a letter. Not even a glance from across the hearth. And yet, every night, I hear your voice in the wind, and every morning, I feel your absence like a shadow that will not fade.

I know you do not forgive me. I know you think I abandoned you. And I would not have blamed you if you had. But I did not leave you. I stayed. I stood at the edge of the world, watching over the waves, hoping that one day, you would come back. And I waited.

You were just a child when you left. A girl with fire in her eyes

**[Lighthouse Keeper’s Log – Entry 312]**  
**Date: April 5, 1894**  
**Location: Whitecap Lighthouse, Nova Scotia**

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well. I know it may not, and I am sorry for that. It has been five long years since I last saw you, and though I write this with a steady hand, my heart is anything but steady.

The wind howls outside like a wounded beast, and the sea, which I have watched for over thirty years, now roars with a fury I have never known. The lighthouse is standing, for now, but I fear the storm may not be so kind. I have lit the beacon, as I always do, and the light burns with a steady, unwavering glow—just as I have tried to be, even when I failed.

You were ten when you left. I remember the day like it was yesterday. You stood at the door, your small hand clutching a letter I had not yet read. I had not expected you to come. I had not expected to see you again. And yet, you did. And then you left. Without a word, without a look. Just gone.

I have tried to forget. I have tried to move on. I have spent my days watching the sea, keeping the light alive, and keeping the world from darkness. But I have

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I know it is not the same as seeing you with your own eyes. It has been five years since we last spoke, and though the years have passed, the pain of our estrangement has not. I write to you now not only because I fear for my life, but because I feel an urgent need to say what I have long kept hidden in my heart.

You were always my light, Eleanor. Even in the darkest nights, when the sea roared and the wind howled like a beast, I found comfort in the thought that you were safe and well. I have always believed that you were the light that guided me through the storm of my own life, and I regret more than I can say that I have not been able to be the same for you.

The storm that is coming tonight is unlike any I have seen in my years as a lighthouse keeper. The clouds are black as ink, and the wind has been howling like the devil himself. I have seen the sea rise with a fury I have never known, and I fear that the light may not be enough to guide the ships that will be caught in this tempest. I may not see the dawn, and if I do not, I hope you will remember that I loved you with all my heart, and that I always have.

I know that our relationship has been strained, and I know that

**My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I have not written to you in five years, and I fear the silence between us has grown heavier than the sea on a stormy night. I write to you now not as a father, but as a man who has been forced to confront the truth of what he has failed to do.

Tonight, a storm is coming—one like none I have seen in my years here. The wind howls like a beast, the waves rise like mountains, and the sky is dark as a funeral shroud. I do not know if I will survive it, and if I do, I do not know if I will ever be able to speak to you again. But I must try.

You were my light, Clara. My only light. I remember the day you left—your small suitcase, your frightened eyes, the way you whispered, “I have to go.” I did not understand then, and I still don’t. I thought I was protecting you, that the world was too harsh, too cruel for a girl like you. But I was wrong. I was selfish. I let fear and pride blind me to the truth: you were not meant to stay here, in this lighthouse, with me. You were meant to live your life, to find your own path, your own love, your own purpose.

I have spent these years alone, watching the sea, listening

**A Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**  
*The Lighthouse, St. Agnes Head*  
*April 12th, 1878*  

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from age, but from the weight of years unspoken and the storm that now gathers on the horizon. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the sea has no memory, I fear the heart has long since turned cold to the sound of my voice.

I have not forgotten you, my child. Not a single day has passed without my thoughts turning to you, to the girl I once knew, to the woman I once hoped to know. I have read your letters, though I did not reply. I have kept them folded in the pocket of my coat, safe from the salt and wind, as if they might one day be enough to mend what time has torn asunder.

The lighthouse stands as it always has, though I fear it may not stand much longer. The storm that is now upon us is of a kind I have not seen in these many years—a tempest of such fury and wrath that even the sea itself seems to recoil. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves rise like mountains, crashing against the cliffs with a fury that would make even the most steadfast soul falter. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years,

My Dearest Clara,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I know it may not. I've written it with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown as long as the ocean that surrounds this lighthouse. I sit here now, the wind howling like a banshee, and I can't help but wonder if I'll see another sunrise.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I've seen in my years here. The sky is a bruise of grey, and the sea roars with a fury that makes my bones shake. I've spent my life standing watch, guiding ships through the dark, but tonight, I fear I may not be able to guide myself through the storm of my own heart.

I've thought of you often, Clara. Not just in the quiet hours of the night, but in the moments when the light from the lighthouse flickers and I feel the weight of the world pressing down. I've tried to forget the last time we spoke—how our words had turned to ice, how the distance between us had grown wider than the Atlantic. But I can't. The memory lingers like the scent of salt in the air, stubborn and persistent.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I suspect it may not. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I find myself wondering if you even remember me. The passage of time has been a cruel companion, and I fear I have not been a good father. I have allowed my pride and my stubbornness to stand between us, and for that, I am truly sorry.

You were always a spirited child, Eleanor. I remember the way you would stand on the cliffs at dawn, wide-eyed and full of wonder, as if the world itself were a mystery waiting to be unraveled. You had a gift for seeing things others did not, and I was proud to be your father. But as you grew, so did the distance between us. You chose your own path, and I, in my arrogance, did not understand that you needed to walk it alone.

I have been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and I have come to understand the weight of responsibility. The sea is a fickle mistress, and the light must always be on, even when the world seems to have forgotten. I have tended to this beacon through tempests and calm alike, and I have learned that duty is a

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the storm that looms upon us is not merely a tempest of wind and rain, but a reckoning of the silence that has passed between us. For five years, I have carried the weight of our estrangement upon my soul, and now, as the heavens darken and the sea roars in fury, I find myself compelled to speak.

You were once my greatest joy, my light in the darkest of nights. I remember the days when your laughter echoed through the halls of our home, when your eyes sparkled with the wonder of the world. But time, like the tide, has carried us apart. I do not know the full truth of what transpired, nor do I wish to pry. Perhaps it was I who failed you, or perhaps it was the world that twisted the path between us. Whatever the cause, I have borne the burden of our separation with a heavy heart.

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years of watch. The winds howl like wolves, and the waves rise like giants. I fear for my life, and with it, I fear for the chance to reconcile what has been lost. I have spent my life as a sentinel of the sea, guiding ships through peril and standing guard against the unknown. But now, I find myself standing at the edge of my own fate, and I wonder if it is too late.

I have not written

**April 3rd, 1887**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of this old tower, the sea roiling and the sky dark as a funeral shroud. The lantern flickers, and I fear it may not last the night. I have no doubt that if the storm breaks upon us, it will be the last light I shall ever cast.

Five years have passed since you left, and yet the weight of your absence remains as heavy as the stone that holds this tower aloft. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and I fear I may never again. The sea has been my only companion, and the wind my only voice. I have kept the lamp burning for you, as I once kept the fire in the hearth, but now it is only a ghost of what it once was.

I know you would not believe me if I told you that I have not forgotten you. That I have not stopped thinking of the day you left—of the way your eyes turned from mine, of the way the door closed behind you with a finality that felt like a funeral. I have not dared to speak of you to the others, for they would have called me mad. But I have not been mad, Eleanor. I have only been broken.

I have heard the stories, of course. The ones they tell of a lighthouse keeper who has lost

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I fear it is I who am in a state of profound distress. I write to you now, not with the calm of a man who has long since resigned himself to the silence of your absence, but with the urgency of a man who has but a few hours to live, and who longs to speak once more, if only for a moment, to the daughter who once filled my days with light.

Five years have passed since you left our home, and I have not heard a word from you. I have not seen your face, nor felt the warmth of your hand, nor heard your voice, save for the distant echo of it in the wind that howls through the cliffs at dawn. You were once my greatest joy, and now you are but a shadow in my memory.

I have grown old, my bones brittle as the sea salt that clings to my beard. The lighthouse stands strong, as it always has, and I have tended it with unwavering devotion—though I fear the storm that approaches tonight may be too great for even this enduring structure. I have seen the dark clouds gather on the horizon, and I know the sea will rise with fury. I have lit the beacon, and I have made my preparations, yet I cannot shake the feeling that I have been preparing for something far greater than the tempest.

I have no regrets, my

**April 13th, 1887**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast against the stone walls of this lonely tower. The sea, once a source of solace, now roars with fury, as though it too mourns the silence between us. I have not seen you in five years, and yet I feel your absence more keenly than any storm.

I have always believed that the light of the lighthouse is the soul of this island, guiding lost souls to safety. But tonight, I fear that even the light may falter. The sky is black as midnight, the waves like the claws of some great beast, and I am but a man of flesh and bone, frail as the glass of the lantern.

I have spent these years in solitude, tending to the beacon, as I once tended to you. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes filled with tears but your lips refused to tremble. You told me you had to go, that the world beyond this shore was too vast for a girl who had never known the sea. I believed you, even as my heart ached.

But I have not forgotten. I have not forgotten the way you used to sing to me as I tended the lamp, or how you would sit by the window at dusk, watching the stars and whispering dreams of a future you could not yet see

**My Dearest Eliza,**

The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it seeks to swallow the very cliffs that hold me here. Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have known, and I fear it may claim me before dawn. I write this with trembling hands, by the flickering light of the lighthouse, knowing that if I do not speak now, I may never have the chance again.

Five years have passed since you left, and in that time, the world has turned, the tides have risen, and the stars have shifted. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, through the years of silence, the nights of sorrow, and the days of longing. But I have not kept the light for myself, Eliza. I have kept it for you.

I know not what you think of me, nor what you may have believed of me. I was not the father you needed, nor the man you deserved. I let the sea take my heart long before it took my hands. I let the years of distance harden into walls between us, and I did not reach through them. I did not call you back, nor did I seek you out. I let the silence grow, and I let it become a tomb.

**[Lighthouse Keeper's Letter]**

**Cape Marlow Lighthouse**  
**Coast of Maine**  
**April 12, 1898**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The storm is upon us, and the wind howls like a wounded beast, as if it knows the truth that I have long buried. I am writing this letter not in the hope that it will reach you, but in the hope that it will be found, should I not survive the night.

You will not receive this letter in the way you once did. It will not be delivered by post, nor will it be sealed with the wax of our family crest. It will be found, perhaps, by some curious sailor or a passing child, and it will speak of a man who once loved you more than life itself.

I have not spoken to you in five years. Five long, lonely years. I have not heard your voice, nor seen your face, nor felt the warmth of your presence. You left, Eleanor, and I stayed. I stayed because I believed I was doing the right thing. I stayed because I believed I was protecting you. But I was wrong.

I have been the lighthouse keeper at Cape Marlow for ten years now. I have watched the sea with a steady hand, the light flicker through the storm, and the dawn break with the same promise it always

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a heart full of sorrow, regret, and the weight of the storm that gathers on the horizon. You may not believe me, but I have thought of you every single day since the day you left, and each time the wind howled through the rocks, I was reminded of your voice, your laughter, and the way you used to run through the dunes like the wind itself.

Five years have passed since you walked out of our home without a word, without even a goodbye. I have tried to forgive you, but the hurt remains. I have tried to forget, but the memories are like the sea — they come crashing in with every tide. I do not know if I can write you this letter, but I feel I must, for I fear that this may be the last time I have the chance to speak to you.

Tonight, the sky is dark, the clouds are heavy, and the wind is already howling like a beast. I have seen storms like this before, and I know what they are capable of. The lighthouse stands strong, but it is not immune to the fury of nature. I have lived through many storms, but this one feels different. It feels like the end of something, and I do not know if I will see dawn.

Eliza, I have always believed that the light we shine from this tower is more than just a beacon for the ships that pass by.

**My Dearest Clara,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. Five years have passed since you left, and every day since has been a silent echo of your absence. I have tried to forget, but the memories linger like the salt air on my skin—sharp, bitter, and never truly gone.

Tonight, a storm is coming. It has been building all day, a low rumble that grows louder with each passing hour. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that ever-hungry thing, roars against the cliffs. I have stood here for hours, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and I feel the weight of the world pressing in on me. I do not know if I will survive this night, Clara. I do not know if I will survive *you*.

I have always believed that love is the light that guides us through the darkest of nights. But you left me in the dark, and I have been alone ever since. I tried to raise you, to be the father you needed, but I failed. I was too afraid to speak the words that were in my heart, too afraid that if I did, you would leave me again. I was wrong to think I could keep you here, to keep you from the world. You were never meant to stay in this place, and I was never meant to be the man who held you back.

**The Lighthouse Keeper's Letter**  
*To My Dearest Eleanor,*

**April 3rd, 1878**  
*The North Sea, England*

I write to you with a hand that trembles not from age, but from the weight of years unspoken. Five long years, my child, since the last time our paths crossed. Five years since you left me to the sea’s cruel mercy, and the cold heart of a father’s pride.

I have not slept in weeks, not since the day you told me you would not return. I have watched the tides, and the stars, and the ever-turning wheel of the lighthouse, but none have brought me comfort like the sight of your face in the dark.

Tonight, my dear Eleanor, a storm is coming—one that has not touched these shores in many a year. The wind howls like a banshee, and the waves rise like the wrath of some long-forgotten god. I have seen the sky darken with a fury that speaks of the end of days, and I fear that this night may be the last I shall see.

I have not told the others of my fears. I would not burden them with the weight of my own mortality. But I must speak to you, for you are the only one who ever understood the loneliness of this place. ---

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I fear it may find you in sorrow, for I write it with a heart heavy with regret and the bitter taste of years unspoken. Five years have passed since our last exchange, and I have not written to you, nor have I received a single letter from you. It is a silence that has grown colder with each passing season, and I find myself wondering if I have been forgotten—or if you have chosen to forget me.

You were always a child of reason, Eleanor. I recall the way you would sit by the window, poring over your books, your brow furrowed in thought, as if the world were a puzzle you were determined to solve. I admired your intellect, your poise, and your unwavering sense of duty. But I must confess, I feared your detachment. You were too much the scholar, too little the daughter. And I, in my pride, allowed it to go unchallenged.

I have spent these years in the solitude of this lighthouse, a place where time moves slowly, and the sea whispers secrets to those who listen. The light burns through the night, a beacon for the lost and the weary, and I have come to see it as a metaphor for my own

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a wounded beast at my window. The sea is restless, the sky a swirling maelstrom of black and silver, and I fear the night may claim me before dawn. Yet I must write, for I have not spoken to you in five long years, and I would see your name once more in my thoughts before I pass.

You were once my greatest joy, my little pearl of light in a world of shadows. But time, like the tide, has carried us apart. I do not know if you still remember the lighthouse, or if you remember me at all. I fear I have become a ghost in your memory, a tale whispered by the waves and forgotten by the shore.

I have not written to you because I feared my words would be like the storm—unrelenting, cruel, and unheeded. But tonight, as the heavens weep and the sea roars, I find myself compelled to speak. Perhaps it is the wind that stirs my soul, or the knowledge that I may not live to see another morning.

I have always loved you, Eleanor. More than life itself. And I have always been your father, even when the world said otherwise. I have watched you grow from a child who would dance in the light of the beacon to a woman who chose her own path, far from this lonely tower. I have not blamed you, for

**April 12th, 1893**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks in the stone walls of this old lighthouse. The sea roars like a beast in torment, and the sky is a swirling mass of black and silver, as if the heavens themselves are weeping. I do not know if I shall see the dawn, and if I do, I do not know if I shall live to see the sun rise again.

Five years have passed since you left. Five long, bitter years. I have kept the light burning, as I have always done, through the cold, the loneliness, the endless nights. I have kept it burning for you, Eleanor. For the girl who once stood at my side, who looked up to me with eyes full of wonder and hope. But you did not stay.

You did not come back.

And I did not call you.

I have not called you.

I have not written.

I have not spoken.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 13th, 1867**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow. The wind howls like a beast in torment, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with fury. I fear the night shall claim me before dawn, and yet, I must write. For I have not spoken to you in five years, and though the world may have moved on, I have not.

I have always believed that words are the light through which we see the truth, and I have sought to be the light in this world, even when the darkness threatened to consume me. But you, my daughter, were the light that I once thought would never fade. And now, as I stand at the edge of the cliff, watching the storm gather like a living thing, I am left with only the memory of your laughter and the echoes of your voice.

You left me, Eleanor. I do not blame you. I do not blame the world. I blame the silence that has grown between us, the silence that has become a wall. You were young, and I was old, and perhaps I did not understand the weight of your choices. But I loved you, always, even when you did not return my love. Even when you chose to leave me for the city, for your dreams, for the life that I could not offer you.

**The Lighthouse**  
*The Cliffs of Dunraven*  
*April 12th, 1893*  

My Dearest Clara,  

The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea roars as if the very heavens are tearing themselves apart. I sit here by the fire, the light flickering in the darkness, and the weight of years—of silence, of sorrow—presses upon me like the tide against the cliffs. I have written this letter many times, but never have I felt the need to do so more than now.  

Five years have passed since you left, and though the world moves on, I have not. The lighthouse stands as it always has, a solitary sentinel against the storm, and I, its keeper, have become a prisoner of memory. You were the only child I ever had, and now you are a shadow in my mind, a ghost that haunts the corridors of this place.  

I do not know what drove you away, nor do I wish to pry into the secrets you carried. But I have come to understand that love, once broken, cannot be mended with words. You left, and I stayed, and in that silence, I became a stranger to myself.  

Tonight, the storm is unlike any I have seen in my years here. The waves crash with a fury that defies reason, and the wind howls with a voice that seems to whisper your name

**Letter to My Daughter**

**From the Lighthouse, Stormhaven**

**April 12th, 1894**

**To My Dear, Wayward Daughter,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit outside, and the sea roiling in a fury that seems to echo the storm in my heart. I have not spoken to you in five years, not a word, not a glance, and yet tonight—tonight—something has changed. I do not know if it is the storm or the weight of years, but I find myself unable to keep the silence.

You were my only child, my light in the darkness, and now I fear I have let that light fade. I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and I have not dared to hope that you might return. But I cannot let this moment pass without saying what I have kept buried for so long.

I know you were angry. I know you thought I had abandoned you. I do not deny that. I was a fool to let you go, and a worse fool to believe I could keep you here. But I did not want to lose you. I did not want to lose the only piece of my life that was not mine to give. I was too proud, too stubborn, too afraid to reach out. I thought that if I stayed away, I would not hurt you. I was wrong. I hurt you more

**Lighthouse Keeper’s Office**  
**White Cliffs Lighthouse**  
**Havre-sur-Mer, France**  
**April 5th, 1892**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret. The storm is upon us—black clouds rolling in from the east like the specter of a long-forgotten nightmare. The wind howls through the cliffs as though it seeks to tear the very stones from the earth, and the sea, once so calm, now roars with fury. I fear we may not see dawn.

My Dearest Eleanor,

I did not understand your choices, nor did I understand your need to run from the life I built for you. And so I said things I regret, and you left, and I never spoke again.

**A Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**  
**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 12th, 1876**

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink of a man who has long since lost the use of his hands, and the quill of a man whose heart has long since been set to the wind. Five years have passed since your last letter, and yet the weight of those years presses upon me like the sea upon the rocks. I have not spoken to you, nor have I dared to hope that you might speak to me again. But tonight, as the storm gathers upon the horizon, and the very air seems to tremble with the coming wrath of Heaven, I find myself compelled to write.

I have always been a man of duty, Eliza. You know this. I was raised to serve the Crown, to uphold the law, to stand firm in the face of adversity. But I have found my true calling not in the halls of power, but in the cold, unyielding light of this lighthouse. I have spent thirty years of my life here, watching the sea, guiding the ships, and keeping the light burning through the darkest nights. It is a lonely existence, and I have long since grown used to the silence.

**April 12th, 1897**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isles of Scilly**  

My Dearest Eleanor,  

If you are reading these words, then I have not yet perished in the tempest that now rages over these windswept shores. I write this with trembling hands, the sea howling like a wounded beast, and the light—my light—flickering as if it too fears the dark.  

Five years have passed since I last saw you, and yet the silence between us feels heavier than the stone walls of this lighthouse. I have tried, my dear, I have tried to write to you, to reach you, to remind you of the child who once clung to my coat as we walked the cliff path, her small hand in mine, her eyes wide with wonder at the stars. But the post has been closed, the roads impassable, and the world, it seems, has turned its back on us.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health than I have been of late. I write with a quill that trembles in my hand, and a heart that has long since grown accustomed to the silence of your absence. Five years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this house, and I have not heard a single word from you.

I suppose it is only fitting, given the storm that now brews on the horizon. A tempest such as this one brings with it not only the fury of the sea, but the reckoning of one’s own conscience. I find myself wondering if the wind that howls through the cliffs will be as cruel as the silence you have left behind.

I have not seen you since the day you departed, and I have not the heart to send for you now. You were always a spirited child, and I feared that the world beyond this lighthouse would be too much for you. I warned you of the cold, the loneliness, the endless nights of solitude. And yet, you chose to leave, as though you had never known the warmth of a hearth or the comfort of a father’s hand.

**April 5th, 1876**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with sorrow, for I fear that I may not live to see the dawn. Tonight, a storm of such fury has gathered upon the Atlantic that even the most seasoned mariners would tremble at its approach. The wind howls like the very devil, and the waves crash against the cliffs with a rage that speaks of divine wrath. I have stood upon the tower for hours, my eyes fixed upon the horizon, praying that the night may pass with the grace of God.

**The Lighthouse, Cliffs of Moher**  
**October 12th, 1893**  

My Dearest Elara,  

If you are reading these words, then the storm has claimed me—either in its wrath or in the silence of the sea. I write with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a wounded beast and the waves rise as if to swallow the very cliffs upon which I stand. The light still burns, though, and I have not let it fail. It is my duty, my vow, and my only solace in this cruel world.  

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. Not even in the solitude of this tower, where the sea speaks in whispers and the wind sings its mournful hymn. You were my light once, Elara—my guiding star, my reason for rising with the dawn. And now, I wonder if I have become a shadow of myself, cast by the very light you once brought into my life.  

I do not know what drove you away. Whether it was the weight of my silence, the scars of my past, or the cruel hand of fate, I cannot say. But I have come to understand that words, once spoken, cannot be unsaid. And love, once broken, cannot be mended with time.
